https://www.nngroup.com/articles/researc:
Where it fits in the research roadmap: Use synthetic users in the early concept and discovery phase, before you write a screener or book a single participant. Ideal for testing value propositions, feature naming, and flow logic for new products (e.g. new loan types, insurance, PIX features) or for prioritising which concepts deserve real user time.

How to run it: Define 2–4 personas that mirror your real segments (e.g. first-time loan applicant, frequent PIX user, older or less digitally confident user). For each persona, prompt the synthetic user with the same scenario you’d give in a moderated test (e.g. 'You’re considering a personal loan to pay for a course'). Run through your flows and copy; capture where the synthetic user hesitates, misunderstands, or drops. Treat this as a filter: concepts that get consistent confusion or drop-off get deprioritised or revised before they ever reach real users.

Operational note: Synthetic output is not evidence. Document it as 'pre-test screening' or 'concept triage' in your research repository, and do not quote it in shareouts as user insight. Reserve real recruitment and moderated sessions for the shortlist that survives—your validity stays in the human sessions, while synthetic users reduce waste and focus recruitment on the right concepts.
https://dovetail.com/ai-content-analysis:
Role in the research process: Use AI summarisation as the first pass after discovery or usability interviews. It should produce a draft structure (themes, sub-themes, candidate quotes) that you then refine—not replace—with your own judgment. That keeps synthesis faster without outsourcing interpretation on regulated or sensitive topics.

Practical workflow: After each round of interviews (e.g. 5–8 sessions per study), upload transcripts to your chosen tool. Use the AI output to build an initial affinity map or theme list; then re-read critical moments and adjust. Tag themes so they can be reused across squads (e.g. 'onboarding friction', 'trust in PIX', 'loan comprehension'). For Nubank, reserve full human synthesis for anything touching debt, fraud, identity, or regulatory risk—AI can still suggest themes, but a researcher should own the final framing and recommendations.

Quality guardrail: Keep a short checklist: 'Did the AI miss nuance or context? Did it over-generalise?' If a theme is going into a shareout or roadmap decision, trace it back to at least one real quote and one researcher note. That way AI accelerates structure and recall; researchers keep accountability for meaning and action.
https://www.userinterviews.com/ai-in-ux-:
When to use it: Run automated checks on new or updated flows before you schedule usability sessions—especially for high-traffic or high-stakes journeys (e.g. loan application, card activation, PIX, first-time onboarding, support flows). The goal is to fix obvious usability and accessibility issues in sprint, so that when real users come in, you’re testing comprehension, trust, and edge cases instead of repeating 'the CTA was unclear' in every report.

How to integrate with Nubank’s process: Align the tool’s taxonomy with your design system and a11y standards so the report speaks the same language as design and eng. Share the report with the squad (research, design, product) and assign ownership for fixes before the next round of recruitment. After each usability study, log which issues were caught by the AI vs. found in-session—over time that helps you tune what to run automated vs. what to reserve for human testing.

Boundary: Treat the output as a pre-flight checklist, not a substitute for user testing. Real users still reveal misinterpretation, emotional friction, and context you can’t automate. Use the report to raise the floor; keep moderated sessions to understand why people behave the way they do.
https://typeform.com/blog/ai-powered-sur:
Where it adds value: Use AI to improve clarity, reduce bias, and shorten surveys for segment and tracking work (e.g. NPS, satisfaction, feature demand, financial habits). It’s especially useful when the topic is sensitive or complex—credit, insurance, fees—where poor wording can skew answers or confuse respondents. Use adaptive logic so follow-ups match the segment (e.g. different paths for investors vs. first-time savers), which keeps surveys relevant and completion rates higher.

Ownership and guardrails: Researchers own the research question and the final questionnaire. Use AI for structure, readability, and branching suggestions; then review every item for leading language, double-barrels, and regulatory risk. Any survey that touches terms, pricing, or legal obligations should still go through compliance and legal where required—AI does not replace that sign-off.

Operational tip: Align with whoever owns the survey platform (e.g. Research Ops or product) so that AI-improved questions live in shared templates and stay consistent across squads. Document which questions were AI-suggested vs. human-written so you can review performance and bias over time.
https://hotjar.com/blog/introducing-hotj:
Role of research: Session replay + AI gives you the 'where'—which steps, screens, or segments show the most drop-off, hesitation, or confusion. Research’s job is to turn that into the 'why' and actionable recommendations. Use the AI digest to decide where to invest in deeper methods (e.g. moderated usability, interviews, or diary studies) and where design can iterate from behavioural signals alone.

Where to use it at Nubank: Run it on critical flows—onboarding, conversion funnels, key product flows (e.g. loan application, PIX, card activation), and support or error-recovery paths. Use the output to prioritise: which problem areas need a dedicated study vs. which can be handed to design with a short brief. That keeps research capacity focused on the most ambiguous or high-impact questions.

Ethics and compliance: Use only where Nubank’s replay and data policies allow; respect consent and minimisation. Do not rely on AI summaries alone for decisions that affect users in regulated or high-risk contexts—always pair with human review and, where needed, qualitative validation. Document how replay + AI informed your research backlog so the link between behavioural data and research questions stays clear.
